sentences.text: Oh, yes.
We stole whole cases of cigarettes also-- 25,000 cigarettes in every case-- and gave some to the Latvians for things in return.
Also, we had the taste for cognac.
And one of the Latvian Jews know how to open a bottle without damaging the cork.
So we were making the bottle half empty--
OK.
The meat.
Oh, yeah.
The meat-- somebody had to go to [NON-ENGLISH], where the slaughterhouse was, to pick up the meat for the SS, and the sausage, and everything for the concentration camp.
Now, Jews would get only horse meat and sausage from horse meat, while the SS would get everything beef.
But it was in the same cases, only the cases had labels, saying horse meat or beef.
So while they were transporting the beef to Dondangen, he would switch the labels.
The SS would have the horse meat, the sausage from the horse meat.
And the Jews would have the salami from the beef.
The same we did with butter.
We've explained that it's not enough butter for regiment to move out.
So the regiment would move with margarine, and the concentration camp would get the butter afterwards.
We felt great.
Also, we knew what was going on on the front, because they had a radio in the PX.
And the Germans turn on the radio, and they left sometimes.
And we played around with the radio and listened to the radio free Europe or Russia, or whatever it was, which was there.
And we know where the front lines were.
We heard the day when the invasion in France was, and everything we knew.
Till July, 1944.
In the morning when we were going to work, we got told, nobody goes.
But two of the Latvian Jews were missing already.
And I found out later on, they overheard a phone call that the-- that the Jews will be taken out.
All the Jews from Dondangen will be removed, because the Russians come near.
And they two escaped to a Latvian farmer, whom they had supplied with cigarettes and tobacco, which we had stolen before.
We were told we are going to Germany.
First, we had to march towards Libau.
We marched only for three or four days.
At nights, we were out in the field.
And the first night, quite a few Latvian Jews escaped.
And they took, although, a number of guards along, which were German or Lithuanian guards.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] on the second day, some tried to escape in daytime, and they got shot.
Then the Hungarian women were marching in front of us.
There were 800 girls from Munkacs.
And we had heard from them what Auschwitz was.
By this time, we know what Auschwitz was.
They told us about the gas chambers and everything.
So Dondangen-- because we gave the food to the camp and everything, we had to feed all the regiment-- had 3,800 Jewish prisoners of that day.
Well, officially, all 3,800.
How many escaped, I don't know.
We marched three days.
And on the fourth day, we were put on a train to Libau, Liepaja in Latvian.
There were guards, who said, anyone who goes-- goes over the side gets shot.
Otherwise, nothing.
Open boxcars.
Three or four hours.
In, in Libau.
And we were put in a farm or leather factory.
The first two days, we didn't do anything.
And there was no food.
On the third day we got food.
One of the Latvian Jews put his head over the wall to talk to somebody else, and the guards shot him.
And he was only wounded.
And [NON-ENGLISH] came out and told him he is not getting any dressing, nothing.
And he let him lie on the ground to bleed to death.
[NON-ENGLISH].
The chief medical officer.
Yeah, because a day later, 25 Jews were needed to work in the harbor.
And one Latvian Jew spoke fluently German with a German accent.
And he said he was from Cassel.
So he went with us.
And I was one of the 25.
Yeah, to work in the harbor, loading ships.
No, there were nothing.
The ghetto was obliterated in '43.
And we worked only a few days in the harbor.
And we got enough food to eat there.
And we found out how far the Russians were advanced and that they were carrying off everything, that they were close to Riga.
They would be in Riga soon.
And we found out quite a few things what we didn't know before.
How far the Americans had advanced into France already and everything.
We worked only four or five days there.
We were put in boxcars to Stutthof.
Yeah, concentration camp, near Gdansk.
When we were came out of the boxcar, SS was standing there with dogs and rifles.
And we had to line up, and they marched us in the camp.
And one SS man told us, this is, see, wire you cannot cross.
If you cross the wire, you will be shot.
On further is an electrified wire.
You'll be electrocuted.
You are in isolation here in this camp.
No, there-- there were nearly half.
Nearly all the Latvian Jews had escaped [INAUDIBLE] and one of the women with them.
Yeah.
The women were separated from the men in, in Stutthof.
But one thing we saw in Stutthof, which we didn't like, a crematorium.
And that was smoking day and night.
Yeah.
That-- there was no picnic in this camp, because when we got the food rations, it was only-- we saw right away that was, something was missing on our food.
And a lot of beating was going on from the fellows who were giving out the food.
Yes.
But I only know the first two letters-- 57,000, something with that.
I don't know the rest anymore.
We had role call twice a day.
Nothing.
They had no work for us.
You could lie down on the ground.
There was not-- unfinished building, where we could go when it was raining.
Otherwise, nothing.
Outside.
A good three weeks.
That a lot of abuse was going on by from other fellow prisoners.
A lot of criminals and a lot of Poles.
Some German, some Poles.
Because they had a green triangle on their clothing.
This was criminal.
And the Poles had a p in it.
And some with a red triangle with a p were also [INAUDIBLE], political Poles.
Yeah.
If they were Poles, yes.
No, I did not make contact with anyone over there.
With a-- with a certain group, yes.
The German-- we had a role call.
And the German SS officer came out and said, I need 1,000 volunteers to go to Buchenwald.
And I know what Buchenwald camp was.
Yes.
Because a lot of Jews from the Rhine district were sent to Buchenwald in 1938.
Yes, and we knew there was also a lot of abuse.
And we knew that Buchenwald was run by political prisoners.
And we said, so we have a better chance in Buchenwald to survive than here with the Poles.
Let's go to Buchenwald.
By freight car.
First, we went into showers.
We were stripped, got other prison uniforms.
The only thing we could keep were our shoes.
From-- from--
From Stutthof, yes.
57,000 something.
No, there was no selection process in Buchenwald.
In Buchenwald, we were stripped and put in the showers again.
And we could pick up our shoes later on.
And that was important for me to get my shoes back, because I had pictures hidden in the shoes.
Yes.
Maybe four weeks.
We were-- we worked there.
We went in this little camp, what they called the small camp, where all the newcomers came in.
And there was a tent.
And otherwise, we were sleeping outside.
In the tent, you couldn't go, because the fleas would eat you up.
Another, elsewhere were some straw bags outside.
You could lie down on [? them ?].
Once in awhile, they would march us to the quarry to pick up stones.
Otherwise, nothing.
And this-- we were there maybe a week or 10 days.
Then Poles got shipped in, 1,000 Poles.
And it came to a big battle between the Jews and the Poles.
Because the Poles wanted the straw bags, we Jews had.
Yes.
And we said no.
And the big fight started.
No, out.
There were no barracks.
Outside.
And then the camp police came in.
They were mostly Czechs.
When the Poles got a beating, I never have seen something like that in my life.
Yes.
And then the Poles got lined up and got told, here are no Jews, no Catholics, no Protestants, and no Poles.
There's nothing here except prisoners.
And every prisoner is alike.
And if you have different ideas, turn around.
There's a crematorium there for you.
We were flabbergasted.
The first time in all those years that somebody was standing up for the Jews.
Between three and four weeks in that little camp.
Only when it wasn't-- during that time, one day we had visitors from the United States Air Force.
27 minutes, the day turned into night.
And it was an earthquake.
The entire factories around Buchenwald, where prisoners worked-- [NON-ENGLISH] was that.
They were building war material.
And the entire buildings where the SS have housing, and administration offices, and everything got flattened out.
The American bombers.
And only two or three bombs fell in the camps.
They tried to hit the crematorium, but they missed and hit surrounding area.
As far as can recall, from the records, what I heard, close to 800 prisoners got killed during that air raid.
And I don't know how many from the SS.
We didn't get the numbers, but we know that quite a few SS got also killed.
A few days later, we were chased in the showers again and got-- first, we had to shave.
We had to put shaving cream on our faces.
And Then.
We got shaved in the showers.
Got new prison clothes.
Got all a number to sew on.
My number was 83279.
We could keep our shoes when we went in the showers.
And then we had to march in what was the movie house actually, originally.
Pictures taken, and fingerprints taken so far.
But anyone who had a picture taken, the minute the flash went on, had to jump off that chair.
If not, he had a needle in his thigh.
To get him off the chair.
We would-- we were surprised how much food we were getting compared to Stutthof, because everyone was getting the same ration.
We were not working.
Then we got selected to [NON-ENGLISH] commander [NON-ENGLISH].
They were making gasoline from coal.
And there were 1,000 Jews were selected to be shipped the next day.
To Troglitz.
That's in Saxonia.
Germany.
Not far from [NON-ENGLISH].
And there was a factory, which had been bombed by the Allies, which were making gasoline, and had to be rebuilt by concentration camp prisoners.
That was my job.
But when we arrived, the camps were only big tents with barbed wire around.
And there were 2,000 Jews via Auschwitz that had come from Munkacs.
Now, the Hungarian Jews were down to bottom line.
There was no life in them.
The only thing what was in them was religion.
I come to that now.
[INAUDIBLE] The Hungarian Jews, they were praying all day if they could, because they were very orthodox.
And they had no resistance.
They didn't do anything.
We, the Jews who came from the ghetto, we were fighting back right away.
We couldn't do much.
I was assigned to build gunner placements.
And--
For the anti-aircraft guns.
And we were getting cigarettes in the camp.
But I wasn't smoking.
And one German-- I spoke the same dialect as he did-- asked me from where do I come.
And when I told him from where, he said, I come from next door.
Then he asked me, you got cigarettes?
I said, yeah, I got 10 cigarettes.
Let me have some cigarettes.
I give you a whole loaf of bread for it.
This is a Nazi?
No, a German soldier.
So I did.
When I came in the camp, I traded a half a bread for 10 cigarettes.
So the next day, I told him, I got another 10 cigarettes.
You have another loaf of bread?
This way, I had plenty of bread to eat.
After maybe four or six weeks, I went to the delousing, which we could go any time you could swindle your way in.
You didn't work that day.
And when I'm lying in the evening in the tent, we had no blankets.
We had body bags we slept in.
My numbers being called up.
And one of the fellow next to me said, you better give me your shoes.
You're in trouble.
I said, wait a minute.
You can't have my shoes yet.
I had a friend.
He was a kapo in the kitchen, a potato peelers.
And he promised me when it gets cold, he gets me a job in the kitchen.
So my number got called up.
And I'm going.
There I see, what they call, the report fuhrer,-- that is the second in command of the camp-- standing there with a kapo and another fellow I didn't know.
Your name, so and so?
Yep.
You know arithmetic?
Yeah.
So they asked me a few questions.
And I answered them right away back.
You come with us.
So I went with them.
You sleep over here from now on.
You're going to work in the food magazine.
You figure out the daily rations.
Oh.
Like you did at another camp?
Yeah.
So in a food magazine, the SS kitchen and the camp kitchen was the same at that time.
But the SS got sausage, came in metal containers.
And you could kill somebody with those salamis, that hard.
So I said to a fellow, we could have some salami too.
He said, there's no chance.
You have to weigh it out, and there's nothing left over.
I told him, you ever heard of putting a couple of pails of water in it?
Overnight, the salami will absorb all the water on [INAUDIBLE].
So you got your salami?
So that's how I got our first salami.
And then later on, they moved the kitchen out.
And they built a new camp in [NON-ENGLISH].
We moved in that kitchen also, a new kitchen and everything.
So we had to steal from-- sorry to say-- from other prisoners to stay alive.
Jewish prisoners, and Gentile also later on.
And also [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the kapos, which we are all Gentile, had special food, better than what the Jews had.
So we were stealing from the kapos also.
Though I worked in food magazine, I was the only German Jew in the food magazine.
The rest were Hungarian.
We got along fine.
But only one thing was bad.
And that happened before I came in the kitchen.
It was the day of Yom Kippur, 1944.
The--
Yeah.
The Hungarian Jews went to the SS and told the SS, tomorrow is Yom Kippur, the day of fast.
Will you shut down the water and not give out any food for 24 hours so that we all can celebrate the day of Yom Kippur?
And we guarantee you that the work performance will not suffer.
And the SS took that with a smile.
No food was given out for 24 hours.
No water to wash or to drink either.
But the work performance had not to suffer.
And how many bodies and unconscious got carried in the camp afterwards, I don't know.
It was a catastrophic thing.
No, it was not a slaughter.
But it was terrible.
And we all, all the German Jews, said, those orthodox should go six feet under.
They should never have a chance to do this.
The Germans were-- the Germans were pushing on us more than anything else.
For work production.
But you've had no water to drink, and you had no food for 24 hours.
July.
No, you mean in the camp?
End of September.
September, 1944.
In that camp in [NON-ENGLISH], Theresienstadt, till April, 1945.
The front was coming closer.
The Americans were coming.
But in the meantime, we had air attacks day and night.
In the daytime, the Americans.
At nighttime, the British.
Beautiful.
Because we couldn't work.
I mean, we in the kitchen had to work.
But the other prisoners could not work.
And they bombed the railroad tracks, which were close to the camp.
And one bomb cart came in the camp.
And one prisoner-- it was an [INAUDIBLE] from Cologne-- got killed.
And we were happy.
And one day, they bombed the town next to it.
And we had to go out for rescue from the camp.
But they had time-delayed bombs there, which started to explode.
And they marched us back in the camp.
But I-- before we marched in, I liberated a goose.
Where did you liberate the goose from?
From a German.
And I twisted the neck and carried the goose into the camp.
Well, I plucked the feathers, and we cooked it and had a good dinner.
Another, another fellow bought in a rabbit.
And we had some cats in the camp.
So we killed a cat.
We had the rabbit.
[LAUGHS] And the fellow got the cat.
From the cat [LAUGHS] for the other fellow.
Some were fair.
Some were very anti-Semitic.
And the camp commander was a bastard.
His name was [NON-ENGLISH].
And there was one ethnic German from Romania.
His name was [NON-ENGLISH].
He did a lot of beating.
He knocked my tooth out.
I run out of the kitchen.
I had diarrhea.
And I bumped into him.
He caught me begging.
And he hit me with a pistol butt in the mouth, and it broke a tooth.
With the civilian, we had no contact.
Keep the Jews on working.
There is a factory going.
Yes.
When the front came closer, the next action was to take us away.
And it was decided to take us to Czechoslovakia.
I would say, yes, because there were over 1,500 prisoners left in the camp.
They had sent out a couple of thousand, which were sick, prior to that to Bergen-Belsen.
And from those who were sent to Bergen-Belsen, there is no survivor.
Because I tried to trace them after the war, and there is no survive.
We were put in boxcars, 50 to a boxcar.
And we were sent via Dresden into the [NON-ENGLISH] Mountain.
We came to a stop.
It was called [NON-ENGLISH].
And we had to dig a grave for those who had died already.
And-- [? while we ?] after we had buried those, we got back in the train.
They gave us some food.
And then the train moved out with two engines.
And then we were maybe a few miles away, the train got attacked by American fighter planes.
